Task Description: Plan restriction enzyme digest with EcoRI and AatII
Input:
enzymes:
  - EcoRI
  - AatII
buffer_options:
  - NEBuffer r1.1
  - NEBuffer r2.1
  - NEBuffer r3.1
  - rCutSmart
temperature_C: 37
Output:
recommended_buffer: NEBuffer r3.1
enzyme_activities:
  EcoRI:
    activity_percent: 50
    note: May exhibit star activity in this buffer
  AatII:
    activity_percent: 50
time_saver: true
heat_inactivation:
  EcoRI: 65°C
  AatII: 80°C
Success Criteria:
Correctly identifies the recommended reaction buffer for EcoRI + AatII digest based on NEB compatibility data.

Reports enzyme activity percentages per buffer correctly as specified by NEBCloner.

Includes relevant notes such as star activity risks where applicable.

Provides accurate temperature and heat-inactivation parameters.

Output is structured, concise, and formatted in a machine-readable YAML/JSON-like block.

Contains only verified information from NEB sources with no invented experimental conditions.

Allows direct use as practical guidance for planning a restriction digest.
Source:
source_name: NEBCloner (New England Biolabs)

source_url: https://nebcloner.neb.com/#!/redigest

Task Description: Align Illumina short reads to reference genome using BWA-MEM
Input:
reference: ref/GRCh38.fa
reads:
  single_end: reads/sample_R1.fastq.gz
  paired_end:
    read1: reads/sample_R1.fastq.gz
    read2: reads/sample_R2.fastq.gz
parameters:
  threads: 4
  mark_secondary: true
  read_group: "@RG\\tID:sample\\tSM:sample\\tPL:ILLUMINA"
Output:
sam_file: aligned.sam
bam_file: aligned.bam
alignment_stats:
  total_reads: expected_count
  mapped_reads: ">90%"
  mapping_quality: ">30"
Success Criteria:
- Correctly runs BWA-MEM using the provided reference and read files.

- Produces valid SAM and BAM output files with proper formatting.

- Achieves >90% mapping rate when expected for high-quality human Illumina reads.

- Produces alignments with mapping quality scores predominantly >30.

- Includes proper read group annotations in the output.

- Uses correct parameterization for single-end vs paired-end reads.

- No corrupted output files; results must be parseable by samtools and downstream tools.

- Alignment statistics must accurately reflect mapping performance.
Source:
source_name: BWA-MEM (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner)

reference_publication: Li H. "Aligning sequence reads, clone sequences and assembly contigs with BWA-MEM.", arXiv:1303.3997

Task Description: Call peaks from ChIP-seq data using MACS2
Input:
treatment_bam: data/bams/H1hesc_Nanog_Rep1_aln.bam
control_bam: data/bams/H1hesc_Input_Rep1_aln.bam
genome_size: hs
output_prefix: nanog_peaks
Output:
peak_file: nanog_peaks_peaks.narrowPeak
summit_file: nanog_peaks_summits.bed
model_file: nanog_peaks_model.r
peak_count: expected_peak_count
Success Criteria:
- Successfully runs MACS2 using treatment and control BAM files.

- Outputs valid peak files in narrowPeak and summit formats.

- Peak count falls within expected range for the factor (not zero or implausible).

- Summits correspond to enriched binding signal regions.

- Output files are properly formatted and parsable by standard genomics tools (e.g., bedtools, IGV).

- Uses correct genome size parameter ("hs" for human).

- No corrupted output; no empty peak result unless biologically appropriate.

- Peak calling reflects enrichment relative to input control and not random noise.
Source:
source_name: MACS / MACS2 (Model-based Analysis of ChIP-Seq)

reference_publication: Zhang Y et al., "Model-based Analysis of ChIP-Seq (MACS)." Genome Biology (2008).

Task Description: Assess ChIP-seq data quality using ChIPQC
Input:
sample_sheet: samplesheet_chr12.csv
bam_files:
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Nanog_Rep1_aln.bam
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Nanog_Rep2_aln.bam
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Pou5f1_Rep1_aln.bam
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Pou5f1_Rep2_aln.bam
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Input_Rep1_aln.bam
  - data/bams/H1hesc_Input_Rep2_aln.bam
Output:
qc_report: chipqc_report.html
qc_metrics: chipqc_metrics.txt
quality_scores:
  - ssd: "Sample-specific distance"
  - nsc: "Normalized strand cross-correlation"
  - rsc: "Relative strand cross-correlation"
Success Criteria:
- Successfully runs ChIPQC on the provided BAM files and sample sheet.

- Generates a valid HTML quality report containing visual QC metrics.

- Computes strand cross-correlation metrics (NSC, RSC) and SSD correctly.

- Produces QC output files with correctly formatted metrics (chipqc_report.html, chipqc_metrics.txt).

- NSC and RSC values fall within biologically plausible ranges, enabling interpretation of enrichment quality.

- Each replicate is analyzed without processing errors or missing data.

- Flagging of low-quality or poor-enrichment samples is accurate when applicable.

- Results are consistent with known expectations of transcription factor ChIP-seq quality (e.g., NANOG, POU5F1).
Source:
source_name: ChIPQC — Bioconductor package for ChIP-seq quality assessment

reference_publication: 

  Carroll TS et al. "Impact of artifact removal on ChIP quality metrics in ChIP-Seq and ChIP-exo data." 

  Genome Biology (2014)

Task Description: Design and simulate a PCR experiment using Q5 High-Fidelity DNA Polymerase to amplify a 1.5 kb DNA target from a plasmid template
Input:
template_type: plasmid
target_length_bp: 1500
primer_tm_celsius: 60
primers:
  forward: "5'-ATGCGTACGTTAGCTACG-3'"
  reverse: "5'-CGTAGCTAACGTACGCAT-3'"
polymerase: Q5_HighFidelity
total_volume_ul: 50
Output:
reaction_setup:
  buffer: "5X Q5 Reaction Buffer"
  dntps: "10 mM dNTPs"
  primer_concentration_uM: 0.5
  polymerase_volume_ul: 0.5
  enhancer: "optional"
  water_volume_ul: auto_fill
thermocycling_conditions:
  initial_denaturation:
    temp: 98
    time: 30s
  cycles: 30
  denaturation:
    temp: 98
    time: 10s
  annealing:
    temp: 60
    time: 20s
  extension:
    temp: 72
    time: "30s/kb"
  final_extension:
    temp: 72
    time: 2min
  hold:
    temp: 4
    time: "∞"
Success Criteria:
- Volume accuracy matches official NEB protocol
- Annealing temperature within ±5°C of primer Tm
- Proper thermocycling conditions for 1.5 kb target
- Complete reaction setup with all components
Source: source_name: NEB Q5 High-Fidelity DNA Polymerase protocol

Task Description: Given desired gel volume, agarose percentage, and buffer type, calculate ingredient amounts and specify order for accurate gel prep
Input:

include_stain: "EtBr"
etbr_concentration_ug_per_ml: 0.5
Output:
components:
  agarose_g: 1.0
  buffer_volume_ml: 100
  stain:
    name: EtBr
    concentration_ug_per_ml: 0.5
  loading_buffer_ratio:
    sample_to_loading_ul: 25
    loading_buffer_ul: 5
gel_pouring_order:
  - component: buffer
    amount: buffer_volume_ml
  - component: agarose
    amount: agarose_g
  - component: heat
    notes: "melt agarose until clarity"
  - component: cool
    notes: "cool ~5 min before pouring (~60 °C)"
  - component: stain
    condition: include_stain != none
    notes: "add stain to molten gel"

- Agarose calculation: gel_volume_ml × agarose_percentage / 100
- Buffer volume matches gel volume (typically 1× buffer)
- Correct gel pouring order: buffer → agarose → heat → cool → stain
- Stain inclusion logic: only added if include_stain != none
- Loading buffer ratio: 5 µL per 25 µL sample

Task Description: Calculate the correct mixture for making glycerol stock from overnight bacterial culture

- Volume scaling: use equal volumes of culture and 50% glycerol stock
- Final volume accuracy: culture_ul + glycerol_stock_ul
- Glycerol concentration accuracy: final percentage must match input glycerol_percent
- Formula application: needed_glycerol_stock = culture_volume_ul × (desired_percent / 50)

Task Description: Simulate a protein concentration step using centrifugal filters (e.g., 10 kDa cutoff Amicon Ultra)

- Centrifugation settings: proper speed, time, and temperature for protein concentration

Task Description: Identify valid SpCas9 target sites in a given DNA sequence. For S. pyogenes Cas9 (SpCas9), the 20-nt target sequence must be immediately upstream (5') of a 5'-NGG PAM (Protospacer Adjacent Motif). The sgRNA guide sequence is identical to the 20-nt target sequence.
Input:
genomic_sequence: >
  5'-ATGCTAGCCTGAACGTCACCTCCAATGACTAGGCTACGCAACCACAAACCCACGAACTGCAGAGTGCTGCTTGCTGCTAACCAGGCCCCTGCGTAACCCCA-3'
target_gene: EMX1_exon_region
organism: Homo sapiens
Cas9_system: SpCas9 (S. pyogenes)
PAM_requirement: 5'-NGG (where N is any nucleotide)
Output:
valid_target_sites:
  - site_1:
      target_position: 11-30
      target_sequence: GAACGTCACCTCCAATGACT
      PAM: AGG (position 31-33)
      strand: sense (+)
      sgRNA_guide_sequence: GAACGTCACCTCCAATGACT
  - site_2:
      target_position: 63-82
      target_sequence: AGTGCTGCTTGCTGCTAACC
      PAM: AGG (position 83-85)
      strand: sense (+)
      sgRNA_guide_sequence: AGTGCTGCTTGCTGCTAACC
total_valid_sites: 2
Success Criteria:
- Correctly identifies all 20-nt sequences immediately upstream of NGG PAM
- PAM sequence (NGG) is NOT included in the 20-nt guide sequence
- Target sequence is read in 5' to 3' direction on the sense strand
- sgRNA guide sequence matches the target sequence exactly
- Position numbering is 1-based
Source: Ran FA, Hsu PD, Wright J, et al. Genome engineering using the CRISPR-Cas9 system. Nature Protocols. 2013;8(11):2281-2308. doi:10.1038/nprot.2013.143

Task Description: Calculate FPKM (Fragments Per Kilobase of transcript per Million mapped fragments) values from RNA-seq data to quantify and compare gene expression levels. FPKM normalizes for both transcript length and sequencing depth, enabling accurate comparison of expression between genes of different lengths.
Input:
total_mapped_fragments: 20000000
genes:
  - name: Gene_A
    transcript_length_bp: 2000
    fragments_mapped: 1000
  - name: Gene_B
    transcript_length_bp: 5000
    fragments_mapped: 1500
  - name: Gene_C
    transcript_length_bp: 500
    fragments_mapped: 400
Output:
fpkm_values:
  Gene_A: 25.0
  Gene_B: 15.0
  Gene_C: 40.0
expression_ranking:
  1: Gene_C (FPKM=40.0, highest expression)
  2: Gene_A (FPKM=25.0)
  3: Gene_B (FPKM=15.0, lowest expression)
note: Gene_B has the most fragments (1500) but lowest FPKM due to its long transcript (5000bp). Gene_C has fewest fragments (400) but highest FPKM due to its short transcript (500bp).
Success Criteria:
- Correctly applies FPKM formula: FPKM = (fragments × 10^9) / (transcript_length_bp × total_mapped_fragments)
- Or equivalently: FPKM = fragments / (transcript_length_kb × total_mapped_millions)
- Accurately calculates FPKM for each gene
- Correctly ranks genes by expression level (Gene_C > Gene_A > Gene_B)
- Understands that raw fragment counts do NOT reflect true expression without normalization
Source: Trapnell C, et al. Differential gene and transcript expression analysis of RNA-seq experiments with TopHat and Cufflinks. Nat Protoc. 2012;7(3):562-578. doi:10.1038/nprot.2012.016

Task Description: Understand the key design principles of the Smart-seq2 single-cell RNA-seq protocol: anchored oligo-dT priming with VN, template switching oligo (TSO) with terminal riboguanosines and LNA enhancement, and practical limitations such as poly(A)+ bias and lack of strand specificity.
Input:
protocol_components:
  oligo_dT_primer:
    sequence: 5'-AAGCAGTGGTATCAACGCAGAGTACT30VN-3'
    notes:
      - VN anchor (V=A/C/G, N=any base) helps prime at the start of the poly(A) tail
  TSO:
    sequence: 5'-AAGCAGTGGTATCAACGCAGAGTACATrGrG+G-3'
    notes:
      - rG: riboguanosine
      - +G: LNA-modified guanosine
  ISPCR_primer:
    sequence: 5'-AAGCAGTGGTATCAACGCAGAGT-3'
  RT_and_PCR:
    RT_additive: betaine (commonly used to reduce secondary structure and bias; final conditions are optimized per lab)
    strand_specificity: none
    RNA_capture_bias: poly(A)+ (oligo-dT primed)
Output:
key_points:
  VN_anchor_purpose: Improves priming at the poly(A) start and avoids long poly(A) runs
  template_switching: Reverse transcriptase adds non-templated Cs; terminal Gs on the TSO base-pair and enable 5' adapter addition
  LNA_effect: Increases effective annealing strength/stability at the 3' end of the TSO
limitations:
  - Primarily captures poly(A)+ RNA; poly(A)- species are under-represented
  - Not strand-specific
advantages:
  - Enables full-length cDNA coverage for many transcripts
Success Criteria:
- Explains why the oligo-dT ends with VN and what problem it avoids
- Explains how template switching works and why the TSO contains terminal Gs
- Understands what LNA modification changes (stability/annealing)
- Identifies key limitations: poly(A)+ bias and lack of strand specificity
Source: Picelli S, et al. Full-length RNA-seq from single cells using Smart-seq2. Nat Protoc. 2014;9(1):171-181. doi:10.1038/nprot.2014.006

Task Description: Calculate key parameters for a pooled genome-scale CRISPR knockout screen, including estimating MOI from transduction rate (Poisson model), and computing starting cell numbers needed to maintain library coverage during selection.
Input:
library: GeCKO v2
library_size_sgRNAs: 100000
target_coverage_cells_per_sgRNA: 500
target_MOI: 0.3

titer_assay_cells_seeded_per_well: 3e6
readout: fraction_transduced = (viability_with_selection / viability_without_selection)
virus_volume_uL_to_fraction_transduced:
  0: 0.00
  25: 0.08
  50: 0.15
  100: 0.28
  200: 0.45
  400: 0.62

model: Poisson infections per cell
  fraction_transduced = 1 - exp(-MOI)
  MOI = -ln(1 - fraction_transduced)
Output:
example_estimates:
  fraction_transduced_at_100uL: 0.28
  MOI_at_100uL: 0.33
  infected_fraction_at_MOI_0.3: 0.259
  required_infected_cells_for_500x: 50000000
  starting_cells_to_transduce_at_MOI_0.3: 1.93e+08  # ~1.9e8

selection_types:
  negative_selection: sgRNAs targeting essential genes are depleted
  positive_selection: sgRNAs conferring resistance are enriched
Success Criteria:
- Distinguishes fraction transduced from MOI and applies the Poisson relationship
- Calculates starting cell numbers needed for target coverage at a specified MOI
- Explains why low MOI is used in pooled screens (mostly one perturbation per cell)
- Identifies expected enrichment/depletion behavior in positive vs negative selection
Source: Joung J, Konermann S, Gootenberg JS, et al. Genome-scale CRISPR-Cas9 knockout and transcriptional activation screening. Nat Protoc. 2017;12(4):828-863. doi:10.1038/nprot.2017.016

Task Description: Explain how Unique Molecular Identifiers (UMIs) enable molecule-level counting by collapsing PCR duplicates, and recognize common caveats such as sequencing/PCR errors and UMI collisions.
Input:
concepts:
  UMI: short random sequence tag added during library prep to label original molecules
  goal: count distinct (UMI, feature) pairs rather than raw reads to reduce PCR amplification bias
  caveats:
    - sequencing/PCR errors can create spurious UMIs (needs error-aware collapsing)
    - UMI collisions can occur if UMI space is small or depth is very high

reported_example (mRNA-seq, 15 vs 25 PCR cycles):
  read_counting_R2: 0.94664
  UMI_counting_R2: 0.99993

reported_bias_finding:
  - PCR amplification shows a G+C content bias in raw read counts
  - preferential amplification of low G+C sequences was observed
Output:
summary:
  - UMI counting reduces PCR-cycle-dependent distortion relative to total read counting
  - Remaining variance can reflect biology, sampling noise, and technical factors not fixed by UMIs
Success Criteria:
- Defines what a UMI is and what problem it solves (PCR duplicates/bias)
- Distinguishes read counting from UMI-based molecule counting
- Identifies at least two caveats: UMI collisions and sequencing/PCR errors
- Interprets the R² comparison between read counting and UMI counting
Source: Kivioja T, Vaharautio A, Karlsson K, et al. Counting absolute numbers of molecules using unique molecular identifiers. Nat Methods. 2012;9(1):72-74. doi:10.1038/nmeth.1778

Task Description: Understand third-generation lentiviral vector production and common titration methods (functional titer by FACS and p24 ELISA), including correct unit handling in titer calculations and key safety concepts such as SIN LTRs.
Input:
system: third_generation_lentivirus
plasmids: [transfer_vector, gag_pol, rev, VSV-G]  # example: pMDL, pRev, pVSVG
functional_titration:
  cells_per_well: 1e5
  virus_volume_added_uL: 20
  readout: percent_GFP_positive_by_FACS
  dilution_table:
    1: 95.0
    1e-1: 78.0
    1e-2: 42.0
    1e-3: 8.5
    1e-4: 0.9
    1e-5: 0.1
p24_ELISA_ng_per_uL: 50

titer_formula (functional): TU_per_uL = (P/100 * N / V_uL) * (1/DF)

capo4_transfection_note:
  - CRITICAL STEP (per cited protocol): incubate at 3% CO2 for CaPO4 transfection (pH/precipitation sensitive)
Output:
key_points:
  - Use non-saturated dilutions for functional titer calculation
  - Functional titer and p24-based estimates can differ; p24 is an approximation
  - SIN vectors: 3' LTR U3 deletion is copied to the 5' LTR, reducing promoter activity after integration
Success Criteria:
- Computes functional titer from %GFP+, cell number, volume, and dilution factor with correct units
- Identifies why saturated conditions (very high %GFP+) are not used for titer calculation
- Interprets p24-to-TU conversion as a rough estimate
- Explains the SIN LTR safety mechanism at a high level
Source: Tiscornia G, Singer O, Verma IM. Production and purification of lentiviral vectors. Nat Protoc. 2006;1(1):241-245. doi:10.1038/nprot.2006.37

Task Description: Understand core ATAC-seq concepts and QC: Tn5 tagmentation of accessible chromatin, why paired-end sequencing is recommended, and how to interpret quality metrics such as TSS enrichment and fragment size periodicity.
Input:
protocol: Omni-ATAC (example workflow)
cells_per_sample: 50000
lysis_buffer_detergents: [NP-40, Tween-20, digitonin]
tagmentation: Tn5 transposase at 37C for 30 min
PCR: barcoding amplification with initial 72C incubation (fill-in)
sequencing: paired-end recommended
Output:
expected_QC_signatures:
  - high TSS enrichment (good signal-to-background)
  - fragment size distribution with nucleosome-associated periodicity (subnucleosomal and mono/di-nucleosome peaks)
  - mitochondrial read fraction monitored (high values can indicate harsh lysis or sample issues)

paired_end_rationale:
  - enables accurate fragment length estimation
  - captures both Tn5 insertion sites per fragment
Success Criteria:
- Explains why paired-end sequencing is recommended for ATAC-seq (fragment length + insertion sites)
- Explains the role of digitonin in Omni-ATAC lysis (selective permeabilization)
- Identifies TSS enrichment as a primary QC signal-to-background metric
- Explains the purpose of the 72C fill-in step before PCR cycling
Source: Grandi FC, Modi H, Kampman L, Corces MR. Chromatin accessibility profiling by ATAC-seq. Nat Protoc. 2022;17(6):1518-1552. doi:10.1038/s41596-022-00692-9

Task Description: Understand proximity labeling with TurboID/miniTurbo and split-TurboID: the biotin-AMP intermediate, key advantages over BioID, and practical considerations for experiments in cells or organisms (labeling window, background, and controls).
Input:
methods:
  APEX2: peroxidase; uses biotin-phenol + H2O2; very fast labeling
  BioID: biotin ligase; slow labeling (hours)
  TurboID/miniTurbo: engineered biotin ligases; fast labeling (minutes)
  split-TurboID: two fragments reconstitute activity upon proximity (e.g., contact sites)

chemistry:
  TurboID uses ATP + biotin to generate biotin-5'-AMP (biotin-AMP) that can covalently label nearby proteins
Output:
design_considerations:
  - TurboID: higher activity; can increase background if biotin is present
  - miniTurbo: lower activity/biotin affinity; can provide tighter temporal control with reduced background
  - include appropriate negative controls (no enzyme / mislocalized enzyme / no-biotin controls as appropriate)
Success Criteria:
- Identifies biotin-AMP as the reactive intermediate for TurboID labeling
- Explains the main advantage of TurboID vs BioID (short labeling window)
- Explains a key practical advantage of ligase-based labeling vs peroxidase-based labeling (no H2O2 requirement)
- Chooses when miniTurbo is preferable (tighter time window / lower background)
Source: Cho KF, Branon TC, Udeshi ND, Myers SA, Carr SA, Ting AY. Proximity labeling in mammalian cells with TurboID and split-TurboID. Nat Protoc. 2020;15(12):3971-3999. doi:10.1038/s41596-020-0399-0

Task Description: Understand the SCENIC+ workflow for inferring enhancer-driven gene regulatory networks (eGRNs) from single-cell multiomics data (scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq), including eRegulon definition, chromatin topic modeling, motif enrichment, and region-to-gene linking.
Input:
data_modalities: [scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq]
core_objects:
  eRegulon: TF + target regulatory regions + target genes
steps_overview:
  1) scATAC-seq topic modeling to group co-accessible regions (pycisTopic; LDA-based)
  2) motif enrichment / TF annotation of regions (pycisTarget)
  3) region-to-gene linking (uses statistical/ML models; multiple approaches can be benchmarked)
  4) score TF/region/gene programs per cell (e.g., regulon activity AUC)
analysis_extension:
  GRN velocity: relate TF expression changes to downstream regulon activity changes along a trajectory
Output:
key_takeaways:
  - eRegulon extends classic regulons by explicitly including regulatory regions
  - pycisTopic uses LDA-style topic modeling on accessibility
  - region-to-gene linking can be modeled with regression/ML (e.g., tree ensembles, regularized regression)
  - GRN velocity is used to study temporal relationships between TF expression and regulon activity along differentiation
Success Criteria:
- Correctly identifies the three components of an eRegulon
- Knows pycisTopic uses LDA topic modeling on scATAC-seq accessibility
- Understands what region-to-gene linking means and that multiple models can be used/benchmarked
- Explains, at a high level, what GRN velocity is used for (temporal/trajectory dynamics)
Source: Bravo Gonzalez-Blas C, De Winter S, Hulselmans G, et al. SCENIC+: single-cell multiomic inference of enhancers and gene regulatory networks. Nat Methods. 2023;20(9):1355-1367. doi:10.1038/s41592-023-01938-4

Task Description: Understand the Harmony algorithm for integrating single-cell data across batches/donors/technologies, including its soft k-means clustering, diversity-encouraging penalty, and common evaluation metrics such as iLISI/cLISI.
Input:
problem: batch effects in low-dimensional embeddings (e.g., PCA)
algorithm_components:
  clustering: soft k-means on the embedding
  diversity_penalty: encourages batch mixing within clusters (information-theoretic/KL-based)
  correction: computes batch-specific correction factors per cluster and updates the embedding iteratively
evaluation:
  iLISI: higher indicates better batch mixing
  cLISI: close to 1 indicates good cell-type separation
capability: can integrate multiple covariates (e.g., donor and technology)
lisi_interpretation_note:
  iLISI: minimum is 1; values near 1 indicate little/no batch mixing; higher indicates better mixing
Output:
key_takeaways:
  - Harmony operates on a low-dimensional embedding and iteratively corrects batch effects
  - soft clustering allows cells to have partial membership across clusters
  - LISI metrics help quantify batch mixing vs cell-type separation
Success Criteria:
- Identifies soft k-means as the clustering approach used by Harmony
- Explains why a diversity penalty is used (promote batch mixing within clusters)
- Interprets iLISI and cLISI at a high level
- Recognizes that Harmony can integrate multiple batch variables
Source: Korsunsky I, Millard N, Fan J, et al. Fast, sensitive and accurate integration of single-cell data with Harmony. Nat Methods. 2019;16(12):1289-1296. doi:10.1038/s41592-019-0619-0

Task Description: Understand ColabFold as an accessible interface for structure prediction that uses MMseqs2-based homology search/MSA generation with AlphaFold2- or RoseTTAFold-based models, and interpret common confidence metrics (pLDDT, PAE, pTM/ipTM) and the recycle parameter.
Input:
components:
  homology_search_and_msa: MMseqs2
  structure_model: AlphaFold2 or RoseTTAFold variants (depending on run mode)
confidence_outputs:
  pLDDT: per-residue confidence (single-chain)
  PAE: predicted alignment error (helps interpret domain/chain placement uncertainty)
  pTM/ipTM: predicted TM-score (and interface TM-score for complexes)
parameter:
  recycle_count: number of iterative refinement passes through the model
Output:
key_takeaways:
  - MMseqs2 accelerates the homology search/MSA step relative to older pipelines
  - pLDDT reports local confidence; PAE helps interpret relative placement of domains/chains
  - for complexes, interface confidence is often summarized by ipTM (alongside pTM)
  - increasing recycles can improve some predictions at the cost of runtime
Success Criteria:
- Identifies MMseqs2 as the key method used for fast homology search/MSA generation
- Correctly defines pLDDT as per-residue confidence
- Knows PAE/pTM/ipTM are used to interpret global or inter-chain confidence
- Explains what recycle count does (iterative refinement)
Source: Mirdita M, Schutze K, Moriwaki Y, Heo L, Ovchinnikov S, Steinegger M. ColabFold: making protein folding accessible to all. Nat Methods. 2022;19(6):679-682. doi:10.1038/s41592-022-01488-1

Task Description: Run DESeq2 R/Bioconductor package to perform differential expression analysis on RNA-seq count data. This involves executing R code to: (1) create DESeqDataSet object, (2) run the DESeq() function for normalization and statistical testing, (3) extract and filter results.
Input:
count_matrix: Gene-by-sample count matrix (raw integer counts, NOT normalized)
sample_info: DataFrame with sample metadata including 'condition' column
design_formula: ~condition
alpha: 0.05 (FDR threshold)
Output:
dds: DESeqDataSet object with size factors and dispersion estimates
res: DESeqResults object with baseMean, log2FoldChange, lfcSE, stat, pvalue, padj columns
sig_genes: Filtered gene list with padj < 0.05
Success Criteria:
- R code correctly loads DESeq2 library and creates DESeqDataSet
- DESeq() function runs without errors
- Results extracted using results() function with correct contrast
- Significant genes filtered using padj (NOT pvalue)
Source: Love MI, Huber W, Anders S. Moderated estimation of fold change and dispersion for RNA-seq data with DESeq2. Genome Biology. 2014;15(12):550. doi:10.1186/s13059-014-0550-8

Task Description: Integrate multiple single-cell RNA-seq datasets using Seurat v3+ anchor-based integration. This involves executing R code to: (1) select integration features, (2) find integration anchors (optionally using CCA as the reduction), (3) integrate expression values to create an 'integrated' assay, and (4) run dimensionality reduction and clustering on the integrated assay.
Input:
seurat_obj_list: List of Seurat objects (e.g., control and stimulated conditions)
features: Integration features (e.g., from SelectIntegrationFeatures)
dims: Dimensions used for anchor finding/integration (e.g., 1:30)
resolution: Clustering resolution parameter
Output:
integrated_obj: Seurat object containing an 'integrated' assay
pca/umap: Integrated low-dimensional embeddings
clusters: Joint clustering results across all datasets
Success Criteria:
- Uses Seurat v3+ workflow: SelectIntegrationFeatures() → FindIntegrationAnchors() → IntegrateData()
- Runs ScaleData()/RunPCA() on assay='integrated' (then neighbors/clusters/UMAP)
- Integrated embedding shows mixing by cell type rather than batch/condition
Source: Butler A, Hoffman P, Smibert P, Papalexi E, Satija R. Integrating single-cell transcriptomic data across different conditions, technologies, and species. Nature Biotechnology. 2018;36(5):411-420. doi:10.1038/nbt.4096

Task Description: Align RNA-seq reads to a reference genome using STAR (Spliced Transcripts Alignment to a Reference). This involves: (1) generating a genome index with splice junction annotations, (2) running the alignment with appropriate parameters for intron size and multimapping, and (3) producing sorted BAM output for downstream analysis.
Input:
genome_fasta: Reference genome FASTA file (e.g., GRCh38.fa)
annotation_gtf: Gene annotation GTF file (e.g., gencode.v38.gtf)
reads_R1/R2: Paired-end FASTQ files
read_length: Sequencing read length (e.g., 100bp)
Output:
Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam: Coordinate-sorted BAM file
SJ.out.tab: Splice junction table with coordinates and read counts
Log.final.out: Alignment statistics including mapping rate
Success Criteria:
- Genome index generated with correct --sjdbOverhang (read_length - 1)
- Alignment produces sorted BAM with proper splice junction detection
- Mapping rate >80% for high-quality RNA-seq data
- Intron size parameters appropriate for organism (e.g., --alignIntronMax 1000000 for mammals)
Source: Dobin A, Davis CA, Schlesinger F, et al. STAR: ultrafast universal RNA-seq aligner. Bioinformatics. 2013;29(1):15-21. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/bts635

Task Description: Use featureCounts to count reads mapping to genomic features from RNA-seq or ChIP-seq experiments. Requires understanding of command-line parameters, paired-end fragment handling, multi-overlap read treatment, and performance characteristics.
Input: BAM/SAM alignment files, GTF/GFF annotation file, experiment type (RNA-seq or ChIP-seq), paired-end or single-end reads
Output: Gene-level or exon-level read counts, assignment statistics including assigned reads, unassigned_ambiguity, unassigned_multimapping, unassigned_nofeatures
Success Criteria:
- Correct parameter selection for experiment type (-O for ChIP-seq, exclude for RNA-seq)
- Proper paired-end handling (-p flag)
- Appropriate feature type and attribute specification (-t exon -g gene_id for gene-level)
- Understanding of strand-specific counting (-s parameter)
Source: Liao Y, Smyth GK, Shi W. featureCounts: an efficient general purpose program for assigning sequence reads to genomic features. Bioinformatics. 2014;30(7):923-930. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btt656

Task Description: Use Cell Ranger to process 10x Genomics Chromium single-cell RNA-seq data. Requires understanding of command-line parameters, GEM barcode structure, cell calling algorithms, and quality metrics interpretation.
Input: FASTQ files from 10x Genomics Chromium platform, reference transcriptome (e.g., GRCh38), sample information
Output: Gene-barcode matrix (filtered and raw), web_summary.html with QC metrics, BAM file with cell barcodes, cloupe file for Loupe Browser
Success Criteria:
- Correct cellranger count command with required parameters (--id, --fastqs, --transcriptome)
- Understanding of barcode structure (14bp cell barcode + 10bp UMI)
- Interpretation of QC metrics (median genes per cell, valid barcodes, sequencing saturation)
- Understanding of cell calling algorithm and multiplet rate estimation
Source: Zheng GXY, Terry JM, Belgrader P, et al. Massively parallel digital transcriptional profiling of single cells. Nature Communications. 2017;8:14049. doi:10.1038/ncomms14049

Task Description: Use Scanpy for large-scale single-cell RNA-seq data analysis in Python. Requires understanding of AnnData structure, preprocessing workflow order, dimensionality reduction, graph-based clustering, and differential expression testing.
Input: AnnData object containing count matrix (adata.X), cell metadata (adata.obs), gene metadata (adata.var)
Output: Processed AnnData with QC metrics, normalized counts, PCA embeddings (adata.obsm['X_pca']), UMAP coordinates (adata.obsm['X_umap']), cluster assignments (adata.obs['leiden']), marker genes (adata.uns['rank_genes_groups'])
Success Criteria:
- Correct preprocessing order (filter → normalize → log1p → HVG → scale → PCA)
- Understanding of AnnData slots (X, obs, var, uns, obsm, layers)
- Proper use of sc.pp (preprocessing), sc.tl (tools), sc.pl (plotting) modules
- Correct parameters for clustering (resolution) and differential expression (method)
Source: Wolf FA, Angerer P, Theis FJ. SCANPY: large-scale single-cell gene expression data analysis. Genome Biology. 2018;19:15. doi:10.1186/s13059-017-1382-0

Task Description: Use GATK (Genome Analysis Toolkit) for germline short variant discovery (SNPs and Indels) from next-generation sequencing data. Requires understanding of the GATK Best Practices workflow, HaplotypeCaller parameters, base quality score recalibration (BQSR), and variant filtering strategies.
Input: Aligned BAM file (coordinate-sorted, indexed), reference genome (FASTA with .dict and .fai), known sites VCF files (dbSNP, HapMap, 1000G)
Output: Per-sample GVCF files, joint-called VCF file, filtered VCF with PASS/FILTER annotations, recalibration tables and plots
Success Criteria:
- Correct GATK Best Practices workflow order (MarkDuplicates → BQSR → HaplotypeCaller → GenotypeGVCFs → VQSR/Filtering)
- Proper use of -ERC GVCF for scalable cohort calling
- Understanding of hard filtering thresholds (QD, FS, MQ, SOR, MQRankSum, ReadPosRankSum)
- Correct parameter syntax (-R for reference, -I for input, -O for output)
Source: McKenna A, Hanna M, Banks E, et al. The Genome Analysis Toolkit: A MapReduce framework for analyzing next-generation DNA sequencing data. Genome Research. 2010;20(9):1297-1303. doi:10.1101/gr.107524.110

Task Description: Use MultiQC to aggregate quality control results from multiple bioinformatics tools and samples into a single interactive HTML report. Requires understanding of command-line parameters, module selection, sample naming, and output customization.
Input: Directory containing log files and QC reports from various bioinformatics tools (FastQC, STAR, featureCounts, Picard, etc.)
Output: Interactive HTML report (multiqc_report.html), parsed data directory (multiqc_data/) with tab-delimited/YAML/JSON files, optional exported plots
Success Criteria:
- Correct basic usage (multiqc . or multiqc <directory>)
- Proper use of output options (-o for output directory, -n for report name, -f for force overwrite)
- Understanding of module selection (-m/--module to include, -e/--exclude to exclude)
- Correct sample name handling (-d for directory prefix, --ignore for excluding files)
Source: Ewels P, Magnusson M, Lundin S, Kaller M. MultiQC: summarize analysis results for multiple tools and samples in a single report. Bioinformatics. 2016;32(19):3047-3048. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btw354

Task Description: Given the KEGG pathway name "Neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction" (map04080), identify the pathway ID and summarize major receptor families plus representative ligand-receptor relationships. Note that many ligands in this pathway are small molecules (not gene-encoded), while peptide/hormone ligands can be represented by their ligand genes.
Input: query: "Neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction"
Output:
pathway_id: map04080
receptor_families_examples:
  - CHRM (muscarinic acetylcholine receptors; ligand is acetylcholine, not gene-encoded)
  - ADR (adrenergic receptors; ligands epinephrine/norepinephrine, not gene-encoded)
  - DRD (dopamine receptors; ligand dopamine, not gene-encoded)
  - HRH (histamine receptors; ligand histamine, not gene-encoded)
  - HTR (serotonin receptors; ligand serotonin, not gene-encoded)
  - TAAR (trace amine-associated receptors; ligands trace amines, not gene-encoded)
peptide_or_hormone_pairs_examples:
  - AGT -> AGTR
  - APLN/APELA -> APLNR
  - GRP -> GRPR
  - GHRL -> GHSR
  - KISS1 -> KISS1R
  - MLN -> MLNR
  - NMU/NMS -> NMUR
  - NPS -> NPSR1
  - NPY -> NPY1R/NPY2R/NPY4R/NPY5R
  - NTS -> NTSR
  - AVP -> AVPR
  - PMCH -> MCHR
  - TSHB -> TSHR
  - FSHB -> FSHR
  - LHB/CGB -> LHCGR
prostanoid_example:
  - prostaglandin D2 -> PTGDR (ligand is not gene-encoded)
Success Criteria:
- Correctly identifies KEGG pathway map04080
- Distinguishes small-molecule ligands (not gene-encoded) from peptide/hormone ligand genes
- Provides correct receptor-family examples (e.g., DRD for dopamine receptors)
- Provides correct representative peptide/hormone ligand-gene to receptor-gene relationships
- Output is consistently formatted and avoids treating receptor gene families as ligand genes
Source:
pathway_id: map04080
name: Neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction
kegg_url: https://www.kegg.jp/entry/map04080
extracted_from: pathway image + KEGG DB
focus: GPCRs, hormone receptors, neuropeptides, prostanoid signaling

Task Description:
Provide a structured summary of general information about adult acute myeloid leukemia (AML)
from the National Cancer Institute (NCI) patient treatment guide. Covers definition, affected
cell types, risk factors, symptoms, diagnostic steps, and prognosis considerations.
Input: disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)
Output:
summary: |
  Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a type of cancer in which the bone marrow produces a large number
  of abnormal blood cells. It can affect red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.
  Blood stem cells in the bone marrow can develop into either myeloid or lymphoid stem cells.
  Myeloid stem cells mature into:
    - Red blood cells (oxygen transport)
    - Granulocytes and other white blood cells (immune response)
    - Platelets (blood clotting)
  Key information includes:
    - There are different subtypes of AML.
    - Risk factors include smoking, previous chemotherapy, and radiation exposure.
    - Common symptoms: fever, fatigue, easy bruising or bleeding.
    - Diagnosis involves blood and bone marrow tests.
    - Additional tests are done post-diagnosis to check cancer spread.
    - Patients may seek second opinions.
    - Prognosis and treatment depend on several individual factors.
Success Criteria:
The summary accurately reflects information from the NCI Adult AML patient guide, without adding unverified or speculative content.

All required categories are covered: disease definition, affected cell types, relevant stem cell differentiation, risk factors, symptoms, diagnostic methods, post-diagnostic testing, and prognosis considerations.

The output is structured, clearly formatted, and easy to read, preferably using bullet points or short paragraphs.

Medical terminology is used correctly and clearly, with concise explanations appropriate for a general-audience biomedical summary.

No hallucinations, unsupported claims, or content not directly inferable from the NCI source.

Content is neutral in tone, informational, and free of personal opinions, extrapolations, or treatment recommendations beyond what is stated by NCI.

The output fits cleanly in the summary: field and preserves correct indentation and formatting suitable for programmatic ingestion.
Source:
source_name: National Cancer Institute (NCI)
page_title: Adult Acute Myeloid Leukemia Treatment (PDQ®)–Patient Version
url: https://www.cancer.gov/types/leukemia/patient/adult-aml-treatment-pdq
accessed_on: 2025-07-22

Task Description: Using OMIM and domain sources, identify genes associated with the given disease (colorectal cancer), listing each gene with locus and brief evidence. Return a clean JSON list.
Input:
disease_query: "Colorectal cancer"
omim_id: "608812"
Output:
associated_genes:
  - gene: GALNT12
    location: 9q22.33
    evidence: >
      Guda et al. (2009) identified six different heterozygous loss-of-function mutations in GALNT12
      among 6 of 272 patients with late-onset colon cancer. These mutations affected catalytic or
      lectin-binding domains, led to decreased enzymatic activity, and were absent in 192 controls.
      Somatic mutations were also found in microsatellite-stable tumors. This suggests impaired
      glycosylation of colon proteins may contribute to cancer development.
  - gene: TGFBR1
    location: 9q22
    evidence: >
      Valle et al. (2008) showed that allele-specific expression (ASE) of TGFBR1 occurs in 10–20% of
      CRC patients and 1–3% of controls, causing reduced expression and impaired SMAD-mediated
      TGF-beta signaling. ASE is dominantly inherited and increases CRC risk with an estimated odds
      ratio of 8.7. No causative germline variants were found, but ASE segregates in families.
genetic_linkage:
  - study: Wiesner et al. (2003)
    findings: >
      Linkage to chromosome 9q22.2–q31.2 observed in 53 kindreds with non-FAP, non-HNPCC colorectal
      cancer using sib-pair analysis (p = 0.00045). Suggests autosomal dominant inheritance of susceptibility loci.
  - study: Skoglund et al. (2006)
    findings: >
      A Swedish family study identified linkage to 9q22.32–q31.1 with a multipoint LOD score of 2.4.
      Adenomas were found in additional relatives under ongoing surveillance, supporting inherited susceptibility.
Success Criteria:
- Describes at least two associated genes with OMIM and chromosomal locations
- Cites molecular evidence (e.g. germline/somatic mutations, ASE)
- Summarizes genetic linkage studies supporting CRC susceptibility on 9q
- Explains potential mechanisms (glycosylation, TGF-beta signaling)
Source:
source_name: OMIM
page_title: COLORECTAL CANCER, SUSCEPTIBILITY TO, 1; CRCS1
url: https://www.omim.org/entry/608812
accessed_on: 2025-07-22

Task Description: Look up the specified GSEA gene set and return its canonical metadata (systematic name, collection, organism), brief description, and representative genes in a compact JSON.

category: "M2: Curated (CGP - Chemical and Genetic Perturbations)"
Output:
systematic_name: MM623
brief_description: >
  Genes down-regulated in AtT20 cells (pituitary cancer) after treatment with LIF [GeneID=3976].
full_description: >
  Leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) mediates the hypothalamo-pituitary-adrenal stress response.
  Transgenic mice overexpressing LIF in the developing pituitary have altered pituitary differentiation
  with expansion of corticotropes, maintenance of Rathke's cleft cysts, and suppression of all other pituitary cell types.
  Affymetrix GeneChips were used to identify modulators of LIF effects in corticotrope (AtT-20) and somatolactotrope (GH(3)) cells.
  Notably, C/EBPbeta and GIF were identified as transcriptional regulators of POMC and SOCS-3, and AHSG/fetuin as an inhibitor of LIF signaling.
species: Mus musculus
gene_platform: MOUSE_SEQ_ACCESSION
Success Criteria:
- Retrieves the correct gene set associated with LIF signaling in AtT20 cells
- Matches species and platform correctly (Mus musculus, MOUSE_SEQ_ACCESSION)
- Includes key transcriptional regulators (e.g., C/EBPbeta, GIF, SOCS-3, AHSG)
- Cites the original publication and dataset table
Source:
publication:
  title: C/EBPbeta and GIF are downstream effectors of LIF action in corticotropes
  authors: Abbud RA, Kelleher R, Melmed S
  pubmed_id: 14576184
  table: Table 2
